sourceRefs.fallbackText: The script Tools/unittestgui/unittestgui.py in the Python source distribution is a GUI tool for test discovery and execution. This is intended largely for ease of use for those new to unit testing. For production environments it is recommended that tests be driven by a continuous integration system such as Buildbot , Jenkins , GitHub Actions , or AppVeyor .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is recommended that you use TestCase implementations to group tests together according to the features they test. unittest provides a mechanism for this: the test suite , represented by unittest ’s TestSuite class. In most cases, calling unittest.main() will do the right thing and collect all the module’s test cases for you and execute them.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The unittest unit testing framework was originally inspired by JUnit and has a similar flavor as major unit testing frameworks in other languages. It supports test automation, sharing of setup and shutdown code for tests, aggregation of tests into collections, and independence of the tests from the reporting framework.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The unittest unit testing framework was originally inspired by JUnit and has a similar flavor as major unit testing frameworks in other languages. It supports test automation, sharing of setup and shutdown code for tests

highSignalBlocks.text: The unittest unit testing framework was originally inspired by JUnit and has a similar flavor as major unit testing frameworks in other languages. It supports test automation, sharing of setup and shutdown code for tests, aggregation of tests into collections, and independence of the tests from the reporting framework.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To achieve this, unittest supports some important concepts in an object-oriented way:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To achieve this, unittest supports some important concepts in an object-oriented way:

highSignalBlocks.text: To achieve this, unittest supports some important concepts in an object-oriented way:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A test fixture represents the preparation needed to perform one or more tests, and any associated cleanup actions. This may involve, for example, creating temporary or proxy databases, directories, or starting a server process.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A test fixture represents the preparation needed to perform one or more tests, and any associated cleanup actions. This may involve, for example, creating temporary or proxy databases, directories, or starting a server p

highSignalBlocks.text: A test fixture represents the preparation needed to perform one or more tests, and any associated cleanup actions. This may involve, for example, creating temporary or proxy databases, directories, or starting a server process.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A test case is the individual unit of testing. It checks for a specific response to a particular set of inputs. unittest provides a base class, TestCase , which may be used to create new test cases.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A test case is the individual unit of testing. It checks for a specific response to a particular set of inputs. unittest provides a base class, TestCase , which may be used to create new test cases.

highSignalBlocks.text: A test case is the individual unit of testing. It checks for a specific response to a particular set of inputs. unittest provides a base class, TestCase , which may be used to create new test cases.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A test suite is a collection of test cases, test suites, or both. It is used to aggregate tests that should be executed together.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A test suite is a collection of test cases, test suites, or both. It is used to aggregate tests that should be executed together.

highSignalBlocks.text: A test suite is a collection of test cases, test suites, or both. It is used to aggregate tests that should be executed together.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A test runner is a component which orchestrates the execution of tests and provides the outcome to the user. The runner may use a graphical interface, a textual interface, or return a special value to indicate the results of executing the tests.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A test runner is a component which orchestrates the execution of tests and provides the outcome to the user. The runner may use a graphical interface, a textual interface, or return a special value to indicate the result

highSignalBlocks.text: A test runner is a component which orchestrates the execution of tests and provides the outcome to the user. The runner may use a graphical interface, a textual interface, or return a special value to indicate the results of executing the tests.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A test case is created by subclassing unittest.TestCase . The three individual tests are defined with methods whose names start with the letters test . This naming convention informs the test runner about which methods represent tests.

highSignalBlocks.text: A test case is created by subclassing unittest.TestCase . The three individual tests are defined with methods whose names start with the letters test . This naming convention informs the test runner about which methods represent tests.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The crux of each test is a call to assertEqual() to check for an expected result; assertTrue() or assertFalse() to verify a condition; or assertRaises() to verify that a specific exception gets raised. These methods are used instead of the assert statement so the test runner can accumulate all test results and produce a report.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The crux of each test is a call to assertEqual() to check for an expected result; assertTrue() or assertFalse() to verify a condition; or assertRaises() to verify that a specific exception gets raised. These methods are

These methods are used instead of the assert statement so the test runner can accumulate all test results and produce a report.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The setUp() and tearDown() methods allow you to define instructions that will be executed before and after each test method. They are covered in more detail in the section Organizing test code .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The unittest module can be used from the command line to run tests from modules, classes or even individual test methods:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The unittest module can be used from the command line to run tests from modules, classes or even individual test methods:

highSignalBlocks.text: The unittest module can be used from the command line to run tests from modules, classes or even individual test methods:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: python - m unittest test_module1 test_module2 python - m unittest test_module . TestClass python - m unittest test_module . TestClass . test_method
sourceRefs.textQuote: python - m unittest test_module1 test_module2 python - m unittest test_module . TestClass python - m unittest test_module . TestClass . test_method

highSignalBlocks.text: python - m unittest test_module1 test_module2 python - m unittest test_module . TestClass python - m unittest test_module . TestClass . test_method

highSignalBlocks.text: You can pass in a list with any combination of module names, and fully qualified class or method names.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Test modules can be specified by file path as well:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Test modules can be specified by file path as well:

sourceRefs.textQuote: python - m unittest tests / test_something . py

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This allows you to use the shell filename completion to specify the test module. The file specified must still be importable as a module. The path is converted to a module name by removing the ‘.py’ and converting path separators into ‘.’. If you want to execute a test file that isn’t importable as a module you should execute the file directly instead.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: unittest supports these command-line options:
sourceRefs.textQuote: unittest supports these command-line options:

highSignalBlocks.text: unittest supports these command-line options:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The standard output and standard error streams are buffered during the test run. Output during a passing test is discarded. Output is echoed normally on test fail or error and is added to the failure messages.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The standard output and standard error streams are buffered during the test run. Output during a passing test is discarded. Output is echoed normally on test fail or error and is added to the failure messages.

highSignalBlocks.text: The standard output and standard error streams are buffered during the test run. Output during a passing test is discarded. Output is echoed normally on test fail or error and is added to the failure messages.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Control - C during the test run waits for the current test to end and then reports all the results so far. A second Control - C raises the normal KeyboardInterrupt exception.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Control - C during the test run waits for the current test to end and then reports all the results so far. A second Control - C raises the normal KeyboardInterrupt exception.

highSignalBlocks.text: Control - C during the test run waits for the current test to end and then reports all the results so far. A second Control - C raises the normal KeyboardInterrupt exception.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: See Signal Handling for the functions that provide this functionality.
sourceRefs.textQuote: See Signal Handling for the functions that provide this functionality.

highSignalBlocks.text: See Signal Handling for the functions that provide this functionality.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Stop the test run on the first error or failure.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Stop the test run on the first error or failure.

highSignalBlocks.text: Stop the test run on the first error or failure.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Only run test methods and classes that match the pattern or substring. This option may be used multiple times, in which case all test cases that match any of the given patterns are included.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Only run test methods and classes that match the pattern or substring. This option may be used multiple times, in which case all test cases that match any of the given patterns are included.

highSignalBlocks.text: Only run test methods and classes that match the pattern or substring. This option may be used multiple times, in which case all test cases that match any of the given patterns are included.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Patterns that contain a wildcard character ( * ) are matched against the test name using fnmatch.fnmatchcase() ; otherwise simple case-sensitive substring matching is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Patterns that contain a wildcard character ( * ) are matched against the test name using fnmatch.fnmatchcase() ; otherwise simple case-sensitive substring matching is used.

highSignalBlocks.text: Patterns that contain a wildcard character ( * ) are matched against the test name using fnmatch.fnmatchcase() ; otherwise simple case-sensitive substring matching is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Patterns are matched against the fully qualified test method name as imported by the test loader.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Patterns are matched against the fully qualified test method name as imported by the test loader.

highSignalBlocks.text: Patterns are matched against the fully qualified test method name as imported by the test loader.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unittest supports simple test discovery. In order to be compatible with test discovery, all of the test files must be modules or packages importable from the top-level directory of the project (this means that their filenames must be valid identifiers ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unittest supports simple test discovery. In order to be compatible with test discovery, all of the test files must be modules or packages importable from the top-level directory of the project (this means that their file

highSignalBlocks.text: Unittest supports simple test discovery. In order to be compatible with test discovery, all of the test files must be modules or packages importable from the top-level directory of the project (this means that their filenames must be valid identifiers ).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Test discovery is implemented in TestLoader.discover() , but can also be used from the command line. The basic command-line usage is:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Test discovery is implemented in TestLoader.discover() , but can also be used from the command line. The basic command-line usage is:

highSignalBlocks.text: Test discovery is implemented in TestLoader.discover() , but can also be used from the command line. The basic command-line usage is:

sourceRefs.textQuote: cd project_directory python - m unittest discover

highSignalBlocks.text: cd project_directory python - m unittest discover

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As a shortcut, python -m unittest is the equivalent of python -m unittest discover . If you want to pass arguments to test discovery the discover sub-command must be used explicitly.
sourceRefs.textQuote: As a shortcut, python -m unittest is the equivalent of python -m unittest discover . If you want to pass arguments to test discovery the discover sub-command must be used explicitly.

highSignalBlocks.text: As a shortcut, python -m unittest is the equivalent of python -m unittest discover . If you want to pass arguments to test discovery the discover sub-command must be used explicitly.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The discover sub-command has the following options:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The discover sub-command has the following options:

highSignalBlocks.text: The discover sub-command has the following options:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Directory to start discovery ( . default)
sourceRefs.textQuote: Directory to start discovery ( . default)

highSignalBlocks.text: Directory to start discovery ( . default)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Pattern to match test files ( test*.py default)
sourceRefs.textQuote: Pattern to match test files ( test*.py default)

highSignalBlocks.text: Pattern to match test files ( test*.py default)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Top level directory of project (defaults to start directory)
sourceRefs.textQuote: Top level directory of project (defaults to start directory)

highSignalBlocks.text: Top level directory of project (defaults to start directory)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The -s , -p , and -t options can be passed in as positional arguments in that order. The following two command lines are equivalent:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The -s , -p , and -t options can be passed in as positional arguments in that order. The following two command lines are equivalent:

highSignalBlocks.text: The -s , -p , and -t options can be passed in as positional arguments in that order. The following two command lines are equivalent:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: python - m unittest discover - s project_directory - p "*_test.py" python - m unittest discover project_directory "*_test.py"
sourceRefs.textQuote: python - m unittest discover - s project_directory - p "*_test.py" python - m unittest discover project_directory "*_test.py"

highSignalBlocks.text: python - m unittest discover - s project_directory - p "*_test.py" python - m unittest discover project_directory "*_test.py"

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As well as being a path it is possible to pass a package name, for example myproject.subpackage.test , as the start directory. The package name you supply will then be imported and its location on the filesystem will be used as the start directory.
sourceRefs.textQuote: As well as being a path it is possible to pass a package name, for example myproject.subpackage.test , as the start directory. The package name you supply will then be imported and its location on the filesystem will be

highSignalBlocks.text: As well as being a path it is possible to pass a package name, for example myproject.subpackage.test , as the start directory. The package name you supply will then be imported and its location on the filesystem will be used as the start directory.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Test discovery loads tests by importing them. Once test discovery has found all the test files from the start directory you specify it turns the paths into package names to import. For example foo/bar/baz.py will be imported as foo.bar.baz .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Test discovery loads tests by importing them. Once test discovery has found all the test files from the start directory you specify it turns the paths into package names to import. For example foo/bar/baz.py will be impo

highSignalBlocks.text: Test discovery loads tests by importing them. Once test discovery has found all the test files from the start directory you specify it turns the paths into package names to import. For example foo/bar/baz.py will be imported as foo.bar.baz .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you have a package installed globally and attempt test discovery on a different copy of the package then the import could happen from the wrong place. If this happens test discovery will warn you and exit.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you have a package installed globally and attempt test discovery on a different copy of the package then the import could happen from the wrong place. If this happens test discovery will warn you and exit.

highSignalBlocks.text: If you have a package installed globally and attempt test discovery on a different copy of the package then the import could happen from the wrong place. If this happens test discovery will warn you and exit.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you supply the start directory as a package name rather than a path to a directory then discover assumes that whichever location it imports from is the location you intended, so you will not get the warning.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you supply the start directory as a package name rather than a path to a directory then discover assumes that whichever location it imports from is the location you intended, so you will not get the warning.

highSignalBlocks.text: If you supply the start directory as a package name rather than a path to a directory then discover assumes that whichever location it imports from is the location you intended, so you will not get the warning.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Test modules and packages can customize test loading and discovery by through the load_tests protocol .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Test modules and packages can customize test loading and discovery by through the load_tests protocol .

highSignalBlocks.text: Test modules and packages can customize test loading and discovery by through the load_tests protocol .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.4: Test discovery supports namespace packages .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.4: Test discovery supports namespace packages .

highSignalBlocks.text: Changed in version 3.4: Test discovery supports namespace packages .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.11: Test discovery dropped the namespace packages support. It has been broken since Python 3.7. Start directory and its subdirectories containing tests must be regular package that have __init__.py file.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.11: Test discovery dropped the namespace packages support. It has been broken since Python 3.7. Start directory and its subdirectories containing tests must be regular package that have __init__.py f

highSignalBlocks.text: Changed in version 3.11: Test discovery dropped the namespace packages support. It has been broken since Python 3.7. Start directory and its subdirectories containing tests must be regular package that have __init__.py file.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the start directory is the dotted name of the package, the ancestor packages can be namespace packages.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the start directory is the dotted name of the package, the ancestor packages can be namespace packages.

highSignalBlocks.text: If the start directory is the dotted name of the package, the ancestor packages can be namespace packages.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The basic building blocks of unit testing are test cases — single scenarios that must be set up and checked for correctness. In unittest , test cases are represented by unittest.TestCase instances. To make your own test cases you must write subclasses of TestCase or use FunctionTestCase .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The basic building blocks of unit testing are test cases — single scenarios that must be set up and checked for correctness. In unittest , test cases are represented by unittest.TestCase instances. To make your own test

highSignalBlocks.text: The basic building blocks of unit testing are test cases — single scenarios that must be set up and checked for correctness. In unittest , test cases are represented by unittest.TestCase instances. To make your own test cases you must write subclasses of TestCase or use FunctionTestCase .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The testing code of a TestCase instance should be entirely self contained, such that it can be run either in isolation or in arbitrary combination with any number of other test cases.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The testing code of a TestCase instance should be entirely self contained, such that it can be run either in isolation or in arbitrary combination with any number of other test cases.

highSignalBlocks.text: The testing code of a TestCase instance should be entirely self contained, such that it can be run either in isolation or in arbitrary combination with any number of other test cases.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The simplest TestCase subclass will simply implement a test method (i.e. a method whose name starts with test ) in order to perform specific testing code:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The simplest TestCase subclass will simply implement a test method (i.e. a method whose name starts with test ) in order to perform specific testing code:

highSignalBlocks.text: The simplest TestCase subclass will simply implement a test method (i.e. a method whose name starts with test ) in order to perform specific testing code:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: import unittest class DefaultWidgetSizeTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def test_default_widget_size ( self ): widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) self . assertEqual ( widget . size (), ( 50 , 50 ))
sourceRefs.textQuote: import unittest class DefaultWidgetSizeTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def test_default_widget_size ( self ): widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) self . assertEqual ( widget . size (), ( 50 , 50 ))

highSignalBlocks.text: import unittest class DefaultWidgetSizeTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def test_default_widget_size ( self ): widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) self . assertEqual ( widget . size (), ( 50 , 50 ))

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that in order to test something, we use one of the assert* methods provided by the TestCase base class. If the test fails, an exception will be raised with an explanatory message, and unittest will identify the test case as a failure . Any other exceptions will be treated as errors .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that in order to test something, we use one of the assert* methods provided by the TestCase base class. If the test fails, an exception will be raised with an explanatory message, and unittest will identify the test

highSignalBlocks.text: Note that in order to test something, we use one of the assert* methods provided by the TestCase base class. If the test fails, an exception will be raised with an explanatory message, and unittest will identify the test case as a failure . Any other exceptions will be treated as errors .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Tests can be numerous, and their set-up can be repetitive. Luckily, we can factor out set-up code by implementing a method called setUp() , which the testing framework will automatically call for every single test we run:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Tests can be numerous, and their set-up can be repetitive. Luckily, we can factor out set-up code by implementing a method called setUp() , which the testing framework will automatically call for every single test we run

highSignalBlocks.text: Tests can be numerous, and their set-up can be repetitive. Luckily, we can factor out set-up code by implementing a method called setUp() , which the testing framework will automatically call for every single test we run:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: import unittest class WidgetTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def setUp ( self ): self . widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) def test_default_widget_size ( self ): self . assertEqual ( self . widget . size (), ( 50 , 50 ), 'incorrect default size' ) def test_widget_resize ( self ): self . widget . resize ( 100 , 150 ) self . assertEqual ( self . widget . size (
sourceRefs.textQuote: import unittest class WidgetTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def setUp ( self ): self . widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) def test_default_widget_size ( self ): self . assertEqual ( self . widget . size (), ( 50 , 50 ),

highSignalBlocks.text: import unittest class WidgetTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def setUp ( self ): self . widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) def test_default_widget_size ( self ): self . assertEqual ( self . widget . size (), ( 50 , 50 ), 'incorrect default size' ) def test_widget_resize ( self ): self . widget . resize ( 100 , 150 ) self . assertEqual ( self . widget . size (), ( 100 , 150 ), 'wrong size after resize' )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The order in which the various tests will be run is determined by sorting the test method names with respect to the built-in ordering for strings.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The order in which the various tests will be run is determined by sorting the test method names with respect to the built-in ordering for strings.

highSignalBlocks.text: The order in which the various tests will be run is determined by sorting the test method names with respect to the built-in ordering for strings.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the setUp() method raises an exception while the test is running, the framework will consider the test to have suffered an error, and the test method will not be executed.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the setUp() method raises an exception while the test is running, the framework will consider the test to have suffered an error, and the test method will not be executed.

highSignalBlocks.text: If the setUp() method raises an exception while the test is running, the framework will consider the test to have suffered an error, and the test method will not be executed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Similarly, we can provide a tearDown() method that tidies up after the test method has been run:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Similarly, we can provide a tearDown() method that tidies up after the test method has been run:

highSignalBlocks.text: Similarly, we can provide a tearDown() method that tidies up after the test method has been run:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: import unittest class WidgetTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def setUp ( self ): self . widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) def tearDown ( self ): self . widget . dispose ()
sourceRefs.textQuote: import unittest class WidgetTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def setUp ( self ): self . widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) def tearDown ( self ): self . widget . dispose ()

highSignalBlocks.text: import unittest class WidgetTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): def setUp ( self ): self . widget = Widget ( 'The widget' ) def tearDown ( self ): self . widget . dispose ()

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If setUp() succeeded, tearDown() will be run whether the test method succeeded or not.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If setUp() succeeded, tearDown() will be run whether the test method succeeded or not.

highSignalBlocks.text: If setUp() succeeded, tearDown() will be run whether the test method succeeded or not.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Such a working environment for the testing code is called a test fixture . A new TestCase instance is created as a unique test fixture used to execute each individual test method. Thus setUp() , tearDown() , and TestCase.__init__() will be called once per test.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Such a working environment for the testing code is called a test fixture . A new TestCase instance is created as a unique test fixture used to execute each individual test method. Thus setUp() , tearDown() , and TestCase

highSignalBlocks.text: Such a working environment for the testing code is called a test fixture . A new TestCase instance is created as a unique test fixture used to execute each individual test method. Thus setUp() , tearDown() , and TestCase.__init__() will be called once per test.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: However, should you want to customize the building of your test suite, you can do it yourself:

sourceRefs.textQuote: def suite (): suite = unittest . TestSuite () suite . addTest ( WidgetTestCase ( 'test_default_widget_size' )) suite . addTest ( WidgetTestCase ( 'test_widget_resize' )) return suite if __name__ == '__main__' : runner =

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You can place the definitions of test cases and test suites in the same modules as the code they are to test (such as widget.py ), but there are several advantages to placing the test code in a separate module, such as test_widget.py :
sourceRefs.textQuote: You can place the definitions of test cases and test suites in the same modules as the code they are to test (such as widget.py ), but there are several advantages to placing the test code in a separate module, such as t

highSignalBlocks.text: You can place the definitions of test cases and test suites in the same modules as the code they are to test (such as widget.py ), but there are several advantages to placing the test code in a separate module, such as test_widget.py :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The test module can be run standalone from the command line.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The test module can be run standalone from the command line.

highSignalBlocks.text: The test module can be run standalone from the command line.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The test code can more easily be separated from shipped code.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The test code can more easily be separated from shipped code.

highSignalBlocks.text: The test code can more easily be separated from shipped code.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is less temptation to change test code to fit the code it tests without a good reason.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is less temptation to change test code to fit the code it tests without a good reason.

highSignalBlocks.text: There is less temptation to change test code to fit the code it tests without a good reason.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Test code should be modified much less frequently than the code it tests.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Test code should be modified much less frequently than the code it tests.

highSignalBlocks.text: Test code should be modified much less frequently than the code it tests.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Tested code can be refactored more easily.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Tested code can be refactored more easily.

highSignalBlocks.text: Tested code can be refactored more easily.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Tests for modules written in C must be in separate modules anyway, so why not be consistent?
sourceRefs.textQuote: Tests for modules written in C must be in separate modules anyway, so why not be consistent?

highSignalBlocks.text: Tests for modules written in C must be in separate modules anyway, so why not be consistent?

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the testing strategy changes, there is no need to change the source code.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the testing strategy changes, there is no need to change the source code.

highSignalBlocks.text: If the testing strategy changes, there is no need to change the source code.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some users will find that they have existing test code that they would like to run from unittest , without converting every old test function to a TestCase subclass.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Some users will find that they have existing test code that they would like to run from unittest , without converting every old test function to a TestCase subclass.

highSignalBlocks.text: Some users will find that they have existing test code that they would like to run from unittest , without converting every old test function to a TestCase subclass.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For this reason, unittest provides a FunctionTestCase class. This subclass of TestCase can be used to wrap an existing test function. Set-up and tear-down functions can also be provided.
sourceRefs.textQuote: For this reason, unittest provides a FunctionTestCase class. This subclass of TestCase can be used to wrap an existing test function. Set-up and tear-down functions can also be provided.

highSignalBlocks.text: For this reason, unittest provides a FunctionTestCase class. This subclass of TestCase can be used to wrap an existing test function. Set-up and tear-down functions can also be provided.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In some cases, the existing tests may have been written using the doctest module. If so, doctest provides a DocTestSuite class that can automatically build unittest.TestSuite instances from the existing doctest -based tests.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.1.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unittest supports skipping individual test methods and even whole classes of tests. In addition, it supports marking a test as an “expected failure,” a test that is broken and will fail, but shouldn’t be counted as a failure on a TestResult .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unittest supports skipping individual test methods and even whole classes of tests. In addition, it supports marking a test as an “expected failure,” a test that is broken and will fail, but shouldn’t be counted as a fai

highSignalBlocks.text: Unittest supports skipping individual test methods and even whole classes of tests. In addition, it supports marking a test as an “expected failure,” a test that is broken and will fail, but shouldn’t be counted as a failure on a TestResult .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Skipping a test is simply a matter of using the skip() decorator or one of its conditional variants, calling TestCase.skipTest() within a setUp() or test method, or raising SkipTest directly.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Skipping a test is simply a matter of using the skip() decorator or one of its conditional variants, calling TestCase.skipTest() within a setUp() or test method, or raising SkipTest directly.

highSignalBlocks.text: Skipping a test is simply a matter of using the skip() decorator or one of its conditional variants, calling TestCase.skipTest() within a setUp() or test method, or raising SkipTest directly.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Basic skipping looks like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Basic skipping looks like this:

highSignalBlocks.text: Basic skipping looks like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: class MyTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): @unittest . skip ( "demonstrating skipping" ) def test_nothing ( self ): self . fail ( "shouldn't happen" ) @unittest . skipIf ( mylib . __version__ < ( 1 , 3 ), "not supported in this library version" ) def test_format ( self ): # Tests that work for only a certain version of the library. pass @unittest . skipUnless
sourceRefs.textQuote: class MyTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): @unittest . skip ( "demonstrating skipping" ) def test_nothing ( self ): self . fail ( "shouldn't happen" ) @unittest . skipIf ( mylib . __version__ < ( 1 , 3 ), "not supported i

highSignalBlocks.text: class MyTestCase ( unittest . TestCase ): @unittest . skip ( "demonstrating skipping" ) def test_nothing ( self ): self . fail ( "shouldn't happen" ) @unittest . skipIf ( mylib . __version__ < ( 1 , 3 ), "not supported in this library version" ) def test_format ( self ): # Tests that work for only a certain version of the library. pass @unittest . skipUnless ( sys . platform . startswith ( "win" ), "requires Windows" ) def test_windows_support ( self ): # windows specific testing code pass def test_maybe_skipped ( self ): if not external_resource_available (): self . skipTest ( "external resource not available" ) # test code that depends on the external resource pass
